questions.title: Primary grouping dimension for patterns
questions.context: move_signals has sender, envelope_recipient, list_id, subject, visibility, destination_folder. Need to decide what constitutes a 'pattern'. Sender→destination is the most common, but domain-level grouping catches more.

options.label: Sender email → destination folder
options.description: Exact sender match. High precision — 'newsletter@foo.com always goes to Newsletters'. Misses patterns across senders from same domain.

options.label: Sender domain → destination folder
options.description: Domain-level grouping. Catches 'all mail from acme.com goes to Work'. Less precise — may merge different senders from same domain.

options.label: Both levels with domain as fallback
options.description: Try sender-level first. If a domain has multiple senders all going to the same place, surface a domain-level pattern instead. More complex but most useful.

questions.title: Minimum signal count threshold
questions.context: LEARN-03 says 'configurable thresholds (minimum count, time span, burst suppression)'. Need a sensible default for how many moves before a pattern is proposed.

options.label: 3 moves minimum
options.description: Aggressive — surfaces patterns early. Risk of false positives from small sample. Good for users who want quick suggestions.

options.label: 5 moves minimum
options.description: Balanced — enough signal to be meaningful. Most patterns from regular senders (newsletters, notifications) will hit this within a week or two.

options.label: 10 moves minimum
options.description: Conservative — high confidence but slow to suggest. May frustrate users who want the system to learn faster.

questions.title: Time span for pattern analysis
questions.context: Signals are retained 90 days (D-08 from Phase 10). Question is how far back the pattern detector should look by default when scanning for patterns.

options.label: 30 days (rolling month)
options.description: Recent behavior only. Avoids stale patterns from months ago. Won't catch infrequent-but-consistent senders.

options.label: All available signals (up to 90 days)
options.description: Maximum data. Catches monthly newsletters and infrequent senders. May surface patterns from behavior you've since changed.

options.label: Configurable, default 30 days
options.description: User can tune the lookback window. Default to recent, expand if they want broader detection.

questions.title: Burst suppression strategy
questions.context: LEARN-03 mentions 'burst suppression'. If someone bulk-files 20 messages from one sender in one sitting, that's one decision not 20 independent signals. Need to decide how to handle this.

options.label: Deduplicate same sender→dest within 1 hour window
options.description: Signals from same sender to same destination within 1 hour count as 1 event. Simple and effective for batch filing sessions.

options.label: Deduplicate same sender→dest within same calendar day
options.description: Coarser dedup. Even if you file morning and evening, it's one event. More conservative counting.

options.label: No burst suppression — every signal counts
options.description: Raw counts. 20 filed messages = count of 20. Patterns surface faster but may be triggered by one-time bulk cleanup.

questions.title: When does pattern detection run
questions.context: Pattern detection is a read-only analysis query on move_signals. Could run on-demand, periodically, or on every new signal. Cost is a few SQL GROUP BY queries.

options.label: On-demand only (user clicks 'Detect Patterns')
options.description: No background processing. User controls when detection runs. Simplest implementation. Stale until manually refreshed.

options.label: Periodic background (e.g., every 4 hours or daily)
options.description: Runs automatically, fresh proposals without user action. Interval is configurable. Small overhead from periodic SQL queries.

options.label: Triggered when visiting the proposals page
options.description: Lazy evaluation — only analyze when the user is looking. Fresh results every visit. No background cost.

questions.title: Where to store proposed rules
questions.context: Current rules live in YAML config. Proposed rules need persistence across restarts but are not active rules. Options: SQLite table (like move_signals), YAML section, or separate file.

options.label: SQLite table (proposed_rules)
options.description: New migration adds proposed_rules table. Natural fit — analysis is already SQL-based, results stay in DB. ConfigRepository stays focused on active rules.

options.label: Separate section in YAML config
options.description: Keeps everything in one config file. But proposals are transient candidates, not user-authored config. Mixes concerns.

options.label: Separate JSON file
options.description: Clean separation from both DB and config. Easy to inspect. But yet another persistence format to manage.

questions.title: Proposed rule lifecycle states
questions.context: A proposed rule starts as 'pending' when detected. User can approve (→ real rule), modify then approve, or dismiss. Need to decide if dismissed means gone forever or just hidden.

options.label: pending → approved | dismissed (dismissed = permanent)
options.description: Dismissed patterns never resurface. Clean and decisive. User must be sure before dismissing.

options.label: pending → approved | dismissed | snoozed
options.description: Snoozed patterns resurface after a configurable period (e.g., 30 days). Dismissed is permanent. Gives user a 'not now' option.

options.label: pending → approved | dismissed (dismissed can be undone)
options.description: Dismissed patterns are hidden but can be viewed and restored from a 'dismissed' tab. Less permanent, more forgiving.

questions.title: Dismissed pattern suppression key
questions.context: LEARN-04 says 'dismissed patterns are suppressed from future proposals'. Need to define what uniquely identifies a pattern for suppression — same sender to same folder? Same sender to any folder?

options.label: sender + destination folder pair
options.description: Dismissing 'newsletter@foo.com → Newsletters' won't suppress 'newsletter@foo.com → Archive' if that pattern emerges later. Precise.

options.label: sender only (regardless of destination)
options.description: Dismissing any pattern from newsletter@foo.com suppresses all future proposals for that sender. Broader — 'I don't want rules for this sender'.

options.label: Let user choose per dismissal
options.description: When dismissing, user picks 'dismiss this specific route' or 'dismiss all patterns for this sender'. Most flexible, more UI complexity.

questions.title: What happens when user approves a proposed rule
questions.context: Approval creates a real rule in ConfigRepository. Need to decide where in the rule order it goes and what match fields it gets. Existing rules use first-match-wins ordering.

options.label: Append to end of rule list (lowest priority)
options.description: Safe default — existing rules take precedence. New rule only catches messages not matched by anything else. User can reorder later.

options.label: Insert before catch-all / review rules
options.description: If there's a catch-all (e.g., * → Review), insert before it. More useful immediately but requires detecting catch-all rules.

options.label: Let user choose position during approval
options.description: Show the rule list and let user pick where to insert. Most control but adds friction to the approval flow.

questions.title: Modify before approve — how much editing
questions.context: LEARN-04 says users can 'modify' a proposed rule before approving. This could be lightweight (tweak the glob) or full (open the rule editor pre-filled).

options.label: Inline editing on the proposal card
options.description: Edit sender glob and destination folder directly on the proposal. Quick and contextual but limited — can't add subject match or change action type.

options.label: Open rule editor modal pre-filled with proposal values
options.description: Full rule editor with all fields pre-populated from the proposal. User has complete control. Reuses existing modal code.

questions.title: Confirmation before approval takes effect
questions.context: REQUIREMENTS.md says 'system must never change routing without explicit user confirmation'. Approval is explicit, but should there be an extra confirmation step?

options.label: Single click approve — no extra confirmation
options.description: The approve button IS the confirmation. Fast and decisive. Consistent with how rule creation works today (no confirmation dialog).

options.label: Approve shows the generated rule for review, then 'Confirm'
options.description: Two-step: see what will be created, then confirm. Safer but adds friction. Good for users unfamiliar with rule syntax.

questions.title: Where do proposed rules appear in the UI
questions.context: Current nav has: Rules, Activity, Settings. Proposed rules need a home. Could be a new page, a section on the Rules page, or a notification badge.

options.label: New 'Proposals' page in the nav
options.description: Dedicated page with its own nav button. Clean separation from active rules. Badge on nav button when there are pending proposals.

options.label: Section above the rules table on the Rules page
options.description: Proposals shown at the top of the Rules page — a 'Suggested Rules' banner/section. Keeps everything rule-related on one page.

options.label: Notification banner + dedicated page
options.description: A banner on the Rules page says '3 new rule suggestions' with a link to the full Proposals page. Best of both — awareness + dedicated space.

questions.title: Proposal card information density
questions.context: UI-02 requires: 'signal count, confidence description, example messages, and approve/modify/dismiss controls'. Question is how much detail to show per proposal.

options.label: Compact card — pattern + count + actions
options.description: E.g., 'newsletter@acme.com → Newsletters (12 moves)' with approve/dismiss buttons. Clean, scannable. Examples on click/expand.

options.label: Expanded card — pattern + count + confidence + 3 example subjects
options.description: Shows more context upfront: signal count, 'Strong pattern' badge, recent subject lines. User can decide without expanding.

questions.title: Confidence display — how to communicate strength
questions.context: UI-02 says 'plain-language confidence description'. System doesn't have a machine learning model — confidence is based on signal count and consistency. Need to decide how to present this.

options.label: Signal count only — '12 moves'
options.description: Just the raw number. Let the user interpret whether 12 is enough. Honest and simple. No false precision.

options.label: Tiered labels — 'Weak / Moderate / Strong'
options.description: Based on count thresholds (e.g., 3-5 = Weak, 6-10 = Moderate, 11+ = Strong). Easy to scan but slightly arbitrary thresholds.

options.label: Count + consistency — '12 moves, 100% to same folder'
options.description: Show count plus what percentage of moves from that sender went to that specific folder. E.g., '8/10 moves → Archive' signals mixed behavior.

questions.title: Empty state when no patterns detected
questions.context: When the system is new or the user hasn't moved many messages manually, there will be no proposals. Need a helpful empty state.

options.label: Simple message — 'No patterns detected yet'
options.description: Brief explanation that the system needs more move data. Mention the minimum threshold. No action needed from user.

options.label: Informational — explain how it works + signal count
options.description: Brief explanation of the learning system, how many signals have been collected so far, and when proposals might appear. More educational.

questions.title: Pattern that overlaps an existing rule
questions.context: If a user already has a rule 'sender:*@acme.com → Work', and the system detects a pattern 'billing@acme.com → Work' from move signals — should it propose a redundant rule?

options.label: Suppress proposals that match existing rules
options.description: Before proposing, check if an existing rule already handles this sender→destination. Skip if covered. Avoids redundancy.

options.label: Show but mark as 'already covered'
options.description: Still show the pattern but note that an existing rule already handles it. User sees the system is working. Can dismiss easily.

options.label: Propose regardless — user decides
options.description: Don't analyze existing rules. Just surface all patterns above threshold. More duplicates but simpler implementation.

questions.title: Contradictory patterns (same sender, different destinations)
questions.context: If move signals show 'newsletter@foo.com' going to both 'Newsletters' (8 times) and 'Archive' (5 times), the pattern is split. How to handle?

options.label: Only propose the dominant destination
options.description: Propose 'newsletter@foo.com → Newsletters' (8 moves, ignoring the 5 to Archive). Simple — user gets the most common pattern.

options.label: Require supermajority (>70% to one destination)
options.description: Only propose if one destination has a strong majority. Mixed signals = no proposal. More conservative.

options.label: Show the split — 'newsletter@foo.com: Newsletters (8) vs Archive (5)'
options.description: Let user see the ambiguity and choose. More informative but adds UI complexity.

questions.title: List-Id based patterns
questions.context: move_signals captures list_id (mailing list identifier). Mailing lists may come from varying sender addresses but share the same List-Id. Should pattern detection use this?

options.label: Yes — detect patterns by List-Id when present
options.description: Group signals by list_id → destination when list_id is non-null. Catches mailing lists that use different sender addresses. Proposed rule would match on recipient or use a list-id matcher if available.

options.label: No — stick to sender-based patterns only
options.description: Simpler. List-Id is captured in signals but not used for pattern detection. Sender matching already catches most lists. Avoids needing a list-id match field in rules.

options.label: Defer to a future phase
options.description: Note it as a future enhancement. Sender-based patterns cover the 80% case. List-Id matching could be its own feature.
